data.title: Good entry point for Berkshire Hathaway after underperformance - Barron's
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) shares have fallen from expensive to attractively priced over the past year, creating a potential buying opportunity even as the conglomerate transitions to new leadership under CEO Greg Abel, according to a Barron's article.

The Class A shares are down 6% year-to-date to $706,000, while the Class B shares have declined similarly to $470. More strikingly, Berkshire has dropped about 13% since peaking on May 2, 2025 — the day before Warren Buffett announced his year-end retirement as CEO. During that same period, the S&P 500 (SPY) (IVV) (VOO) has returned 26%, creating a nearly 40-percentage-point performance gap.

The stock now trades below 1.4x estimated Q1 book value, compared to 1.8x a year ago. Longtime Berkshire analyst Chris Bloomstran of Semper Augustus Investments estimates intrinsic value at $855,000 per share - 21% above current levels.

Investor Christopher Davis of Hudson Value Partners calls Berkshire “the ultimate HALO company (heavy assets, low obsolescence),” citing the durability of its insurance business and hard-to-replicate industrial operations including Burlington Northern Santa Fe railroad and Berkshire Hathaway Energy.

UBS analyst Brian Meredith maintains a Buy rating with an $871,000 price target, noting the stock is attractively priced relative to intrinsic value and defensive given its diversified earnings power and $373 billion cash position.

Berkshire resumed share repurchases on March 4 after a nearly two-year hiatus, buying more than $200M of stock. With shares down since then, the company may have accelerated the program.

The company reports earnings on May 2, coinciding with its annual meeting.

data.title: Trending stocks this week as markets weigh war risks and corporate results
data.description:
Wall Street’s major averages ended the week mixed, even as the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq Composite climbed to fresh highs as the unresolved conflict in the Middle East pushed oil prices higher.

Crude oil futures rose about 13.2% this week to settle near $94 a barrel, while Brent crude gained 17.2% to around $105 a barrel as of post-market Friday.

On the economic front, retail sales increased more than expected in March, while February business inventories rose 0.4% month over month to $2.69 trillion, ahead of the 0.2% consensus estimate, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

Meanwhile, earnings season gained momentum with a busy slate of reports across sectors from companies including Tesla, Intel, ServiceNow, and defense-related names such as GE Aerospace, Boeing, Honeywell, 3M, Union Pacific, and Lockheed Martin.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) gained +0.6%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) rose +1.5% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) declined -0.4%.

Here’s what caught investor attention this week:

Tesla (TSLA) shares were under pressure Thursday after the company’s Q1 earnings report. The electric vehicle giant reported revenue of $22.9B (+15.8% year-over-year). EPS came in at $0.41 vs. $0.35 consensus and $0.12 a year ago. During the earnings conference call, CEO Elon Musk said 2026 will be a very exciting year. He said the company's heavy capital expenditure spending of about $25B this year will pay off in the future.

Meta Platforms (META) announced an expanded deal with Amazon (AMZN) to deploy "tens of millions" of AWS's Graviton processors to power its AI. Financial details of the deal were not made public, but it is expected to last for at least three years.

Intel (INTC) shares rallied after the chipmaker posted first-quarter results and guidance that topped Wall Street expectations. For the quarter ended March 28, Intel reported adjusted earnings of $0.29 per share on revenue of $13.58B, up 7.2% year over year, while guidance also came in ahead of expectations. Looking ahead, Intel expects second-quarter revenue to be between $13.8 and $14.8B and an adjusted $0.20 per share.

ServiceNow (NOW) dropped over 17% on Thursday after its quarterly report showed slightly lower-than-expected margins. For the full year, ServiceNow sees a subscription-adjusted gross margin of 81.5%, below the 82.1% estimate, citing recent acquisitions, with normalization expected by 2027.

Apple (AAPL) announced a major leadership transition on April 21, with Tim Cook stepping down as CEO after a 15-year tenure that saw the company’s stock appreciate more than 10x. John Ternus, currently head of hardware engineering, will assume the CEO role on Sept. 1, while Cook transitions to the position of executive chairman.

Lockheed Martin (LMT) was down 4% in early trading on Thursday after the largest U.S. defense contractor and maker of fighter jets, missile systems, and military spacecraft reported dour results, missing Wall Street expectations for both revenue and earnings. Revenue was essentially flat at $17.96B in the first quarter, below analysts’ consensus estimate of $18.25B. Adjusted earnings came in at $6.44 a share, below Wall Street expectations, pressured by unfavorable profit adjustments on the F-16 and C-130 programs as well as weaker margins in the Space unit.

Psychedelic-related stocks rose after President Donald Trump signed an executive order aimed at accelerating research and expanding access to these substances to treat post-traumatic stress disorder. Compass Pathways (CMPS) rose more than 42% on Monday, while AtaiBeckley (ATAI) climbed almost 22%.

Charter Communications (CHTR) fell on Friday after the company released its first-quarter results that included a larger-than-expected loss of broadband customers. The parent company of Spectrum mobile and internet services added 368K new mobile lines (versus +432K estimates) during the quarter but lost 120K Spectrum Internet customers (versus -100K estimates) amid increased promotions from its competitors.

UnitedHealth (UNH) rose after the healthcare company reported better-than-expected Q1 results and increased its full-year earnings outlook. Q1 revenue grew 2% Y/Y to $111.7B, exceeding the consensus by $2.1B, thanks mainly to its insurance segment. Adj. EPS reached $7.23, largely unchanged from Q4 2025 but beating Street forecasts by $0.63.

Marvell Technology (MRVL) gained on Monday after it was reported that the specialty semiconductor company may work with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) on developing future artificial intelligence chips.

POET Technologies (POET) soared 105.7% over the week after the firm confirmed a business tie-up with Marvell Technologies in an interview with Stocktwits. CFO Thomas Mika told Stocktwits that the company had received a purchase order tied to Marvell (MRVL) and was awaiting responses on additional orders from manufacturing partners and customers, including Foxconn (FXCOF) (HNHAF) (HNHPF) and Luxshare Precision.

Amazon (AMZN)-backed nuclear startup X-Energy (XE) raised nearly $1.02B in an upsized US initial public offering that priced above the marketed range. The Maryland-based company priced more than 44.25M shares at $23 each, above its marketed range of $16 to $19 per share. At that price, the company is valued at about $9.1B based on outstanding shares listed in its filings.

data.title: Real estate stocks underperform as earnings season picks up steam
data.description:
This week, real estate stocks underperformed broader markets as the earnings season picked up steam.

The S&P 500 Real Estate Index Sector (SP500-60) fell 1.41% to 278.83 points, while the accompanying State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE) declined 1.42% to $43.83. The broader S&P 500 index outperformed with a 0.47% increase to 7,165.08 points.

The Dow Jones REIT Indx Equity REIT Total Return Index (REIT:IND) retreated 1.32%, while the FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs index was down 1.30%.

Here is a look at the week's notable earnings releases:

  • Digital Realty Trust (DLR) - FFO beats, Revenue beats
  • Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) - FFO beats, Revenue beats
  • Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI) - FFO beats, Revenue beats
  • Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE) - FFO beats, Revenue beats
  • CBRE Group (CBRE) - EPS beats, Revenue beats
  • Crown Castle (CCI) - FFO beats, Revenue beats
  • Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) - FFO beats, Revenue beats
  • Getty Realty (GTY) - FFO beats, Revenue in-line
  • Agree Realty (ADC) - FFO beats, Revenue beats

Weekly Winners & Losers

Among largecap stocks, CoStar Group (CSGP) led the weekly losers, with an 8.26% decline to $36.44. The real estate services provider is set to post its first-quarter earnings next week.

Healthpeak Properties (DOC) followed, retreating 5.41% to close at $16.43.

Equinix (EQIX) (+1.85% W/W to $1,108.76) and AGNC Investment (AGNC) (+1.10% W/W to $11.02) topped the gainers.

AGNC posted stronger-than-expected Q1 earnings this week even as its book value dipped due to volatility in the mortgage-backed securities market at the end of the quarter.

For the midcap losers, Fermi (FRMI) was the biggest decliner, retreating 17.56% to close at $5.40. The stock dropped amid a short call from Fuzzy Panda Research and after the company announced that its CEO and CFO were stepping down.

National Health Investors (NHI) (-10.10% W/W to $77.00) was the next on the list. This week, the health care REIT announced the retirement of CFO John Spaid.

Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) (+4.16% W/W to $5.51), The St. Joe Company (JOE) (+3.94% W/W to $71.01), and Cousins Properties (CUZ) (+3.36% W/W to $24.90) were the top gainers.

Sila Realty Trust (SILA) was the biggest gainer among smallcap stocks, adding 19.08% to $30.40. The week saw SILA announce a definitive merger agreement with certain affiliates of Blue Owl Capital (OWL) involving an all-cash transaction valued at ~$2.4B.

Industrial Logistics Properties Trust (ILPT) (+14.88% W/W to $7.41) followed. The stock benefited from a company announcement about a $1.62B five-year, interest-only mortgage financing at a 5.71% fixed rate.

KKR Real Estate Finance Trust (KREF) was the biggest loser in the category. The stock plunged 10.16% to $6.01 on Q1 earnings miss.

Here is a look at the subsector performances for the week:

data.title: Insider trades: Morgan Stanley, Uber, Adobe among notable names
data.description:
This week insider trades were seen across major firms like Uber (UBER) and Morgan Stanley (MS). The following transactions occurred between April 20, 2026, and April 24, 2026.

Morgan Stanley (MS) Chief Legal and Administrative Officer Eric Grossman sold 11,118 shares at a price range of $190 to $191.5 per share for a total value of $2.12M, reducing his direct holdings by 6.16% to 169,403 shares.

Adobe (ADBE) EVP and CFO Daniel Durn disposed of 3.02% of his direct holdings in the firm. He sold 1,336 shares at $248.02 per share for $331,355, trimming his stake to 42,833 shares.

Tilray Brands (TLRY) director David Hopkinson sold 33,556 shares at $6.94 per share for $232,879, fully exiting his direct position.

U.S. Bancorp (USB) Vice Chair Jodi Richard sold 40,000 shares, reducing her direct holdings by 16.18%. She sold each share at $57.00, totaling $2.28M and reducing her stake to 207,251 shares.

Fluor Corp. disposed of 13.5M shares of NuScale Power (SMR) at $11.81 each for proceeds of $159.4 million, completely exiting its stake in the company.

On the purchase side, Uber (UBER) revealed that its holdings in EV maker Lucid have increased to 37.75M shares, representing an 11.52% stake in the company. It holds the stake indirectly through its wholly owned subsidiary, SMB Holding Corporation.

Vladimir Galkin revealed in a filing that he holds 37.2M shares of JetBlue (JBLU), translating to a 10% stake in the firm.

Baozun (BZUN) CEO Wenbin Qiu purchased 16,000 shares of the company for $3.14 each for a total value of $50,240. The CEO’s indirect holdings have risen to 1.43M shares.

data.title: Earnings scoreboard for financials: 18 of 19 companies see Y/Y growth in earnings
data.description:
Most financial companies from the S&P 500 delivered strong profit performance in the latest earnings season, with 15 out of 19 firms beating EPS estimates while 5 trailed estimates.

On the other hand, based on the revenue, performance was more mixed. 10 companies beat revenue estimates, while 9 companies fell short of estimates.

This structure clearly indicates that profitability remains strong, while top-line growth is uneven across the sector. Of the 19 financial companies of the S&P 500 index, 18 reported Y/Y earnings growth, and 15 saw topline growth.

Here is a summary of recent financial results from top financial stocks:

American Express (AXP) reported a revenue beat and EPS beat, driven by strong card spending and resilient demand from premium customers. Margins held steady, and the company reaffirmed its full-year outlook, signaling continued confidence in consumer strength.

Blackstone (BX) posted a revenue beat with Q1 distributable EPS of $1.36, vs. the consensus of $1.34, increased from $1.75 in Q4 2025 and $1.09 in last year’s Q1. Infrastructure and Tactical Opportunities turned in the strongest returns of the quarter, while its Liquid Credit and Opportunistic Real Estate strategies had negative returns.

Capital One Financial (COF) as Q1 earnings and revenue came in lighter than the Wall Street consensus estimates. The credit card lender continued to integrate Discover's operations into its business.

Nasdaq (NDAQ) reported a revenue beat and EPS beat, supported by strong performance driven by growth at the fintech and index segments, even as expenses continued to swell.

Interactive Brokers (IBKR) reported Q1 revenue of about $1.67B, slightly missing estimates, while EPS of $0.60 came in below or in line with expectations. Trading activity remained strong, but lower regulatory fees and some pressure on commissions weighed on overall results.

Synchrony Financial (SYF) Q1 earnings exceeded the Wall Street consensus on Tuesday as its lower provision for credit losses offset a Q/Q decline in net interest income. It also announced a $6.5B stock repurchase authorization, and the board approved a planned 13% dividend increase to $0.34 per share, beginning in Q3 2026.

Moody's (MCO) Q1 earnings exceeded the Wall Street consensus on strong results in its investor services and analytics divisions. The company reaffirmed its 2026 guidance for revenue growth in the high-single-digit percent range and adjusted EPS of $16.40-$17.00.

Chubb (CB) Q1 GAAP EPS fell short of the average analyst estimate, while net realized losses jumped. Net premiums written increased 10.7% year-over-year to $14.01B.

At the industry level, 3 consumer finance banks, 4 exchanges and data firms, 3 asset management companies, 4 property and casualty insurance names, 2 investment banking and brokerage firms, 1 regional bank, and 2 life insurance companies reported earnings this week.

Among the financial companies that are reporting earnings next week are Mastercard (MA), Robinhood Markets (HOOD), S&P 500 (SPGI), Visa (V), The Allstate Corporation (ALL), American International Group (AIG), Aflac (AFL), and T. Rowe Price Group (TROW).

data.title: Top 8 stories: What happened in crypto market this week & what's next?
data.description:
This week in the cryptocurrency market wasn’t just noise but more like a shift in momentum. Policymakers stepped in with clearer intent, institutions doubled down, and Bitcoin (BTC-USD) quietly touched a ~$79K monthly high.

In this article, we break down what happened in the crypto market this week, while the current global market cap stands at ~$2.6T.

  1. Bipartisan push for crypto rules gains pace: Senator Cynthia Lummis said there’s now bipartisan backing in Congress for a crypto market structure bill. That’s one of the biggest crypto market events because clearer rules could finally reduce uncertainty that’s been holding institutions back, reported Watcher Guru.
  2. BlackRock & Strategy's big bet: BlackRock’s iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT) saw $709.76M in inflows this week alone. Meanwhile, Strategy (MSTR) bought 34.16K BTC for ~$2.54B, taking total holdings to 815.06K Bitcoin (BTC-USD).
  3. U.S. military testing Bitcoin network: Admiral Samuel Paparo reportedly confirmed the U.S. military is running a Bitcoin (BTC-USD) node and testing how the Bitcoin network can help secure and protect its systems.
  4. Russia opens the door for crypto in global trade: Russia passed a bill allowing businesses to use crypto for cross-border payments, even under sanctions. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and Ethereum (ETH-USD) are expected to be the first cryptocurrencies allowed under this system.
  5. South Africa tightens crypto movement rules: South Africa plans strict crypto rules—big transactions must go through approved platforms, cross-border transfers need approval, and foreign holdings must be declared. Breaking rules could lead to asset seizure, up to an R1M fine, or 5 years in jail.
  6. Tether freezes $344M USDT in crackdown: Tether (USDT-USD) froze over $344M USDT in coordination with U.S. authorities and the Office of Foreign Assets Control. It highlights the growing role of stablecoins in compliance frameworks.
  7. Tesla takes a $222M hit on Bitcoin valuation: Tesla (TSLA) Q1 2026 revenue rose 16% YoY to $22.38B, with strong profits and $1.44B free cash flow. The company didn’t buy or sell Bitcoin (BTC-USD), but its digital asset value fell by ~$222M to $786M due to a decline in BTC prices.
  8. Coinbase & Bybit explore tokenized U.S. stocks: Coinbase (COIN) is in talks with Bybit to bring U.S. stocks on-chain, including pre-IPO shares, using tokenization and global distribution. Talks don’t include any investment or U.S. entry for Bybit.

What to Watch Next?

  • Donald Trump speaking at a crypto conference—expect policy signals or market-moving commentary.
  • Federal Reserve meeting on April 28–29.
  • DOJ expected to drop criminal investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell.
  • Fed balance sheet update release—key for tracking liquidity direction.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: CrowdStrike, AMD and Sandisk among top picks
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) closed in the green on Friday after the week saw a flurry of corporate earnings from Tesla, Intel, and United Airlines.

For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) rose marginally, while Dow (DJI) lost 0.3%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

CrowdStrike upgraded on AI tailwinds

KeyBanc Capital Markets upgraded CrowdStrike (CRWD) to Overweight and establish a $525 price target, citing expected tailwinds from artificial intelligence.

The brokerage said it upgraded as it sees “Mythos as a catalyst for accelerating security budgets, the breadth and depth of the Falcon platform well positioned to capitalize on near-term hygiene priorities and long-term runtime defense requirements, strong defensibility to AI-disruption risk, and an incremental competitive advantage from its Glasswing inclusion.

Other short-term positives for CrowdStrike include its Falcon platform, which should get more attention ahead of any risk coming from Anthropic's Mythos model, as well as increased customer engagement, it said.

AMD upgraded on CPU explosion

AMD (AMD) was upgraded at D.A. Davidson, as the investment firm cited a “structural increase” in CPU demand, due to agentic artificial intelligence.

“We are upgrading shares of AMD to Buy from Neutral and raising our price target to $375 from $220 based on a structural increase in CPU demand on top of much improved visibility into AMD's role in the great data center buildout,” analyst Gil Luria wrote in a note to clients. “We believe that given the magnitude of Intel's beat, there is meaningful upside to AMD's estimates, starting with the March quarter which AMD is due to report May 5th.”

“We view Intel's results as a precursor for a huge step-up for AMD's CPU franchise and believe the structural shift towards agentic AI workloads is creating unprecedented demand for server CPUs," Luria added.

Sandisk gets bullish rating at GF Securities ahead of earnings

Sandisk (SNDK) was upgraded to Buy at GF Securities, with the investment firm putting a $1,277 price target on the stock.

“SNDK will report FY3Q26 revenue on April 30, and we expect a strong result and guidance given recent upside surprise from consumer electronics,” analyst Yang Zhou wrote. The brokerage forecast SNDK’s FY3Q revenue at $4.9B and margin at 71.5% with FY4Q26 guidance [greater than] $10B and margin [greater than] 84%.

“We expect another catalyst to be the increase in liabilities, suggesting meaningful down payment of LTAs,” it added.

Morgan Stanley upgraded Phillips 66 (PSX) to Overweight from Equal Weight with a $174 price target, raised from $147, pointing to chemicals as a key differentiator for the refiner and noting increasingly attractive valuation.

Morgan Stanley downgraded Zscaler's (ZS) rating to Equal-weight from Overweight and reduced the price target to $155 from $200, citing growing competition in Secure Access Service Edge, or SASE.

Analysts led by Meta Marshall said that when they upgraded Zscaler to Overweight last year, they did so on the view that the company could expand its platform.

"While we have seen early traction here, with ZDX bookings up 80% Y/Y to $100mm ARR, we have not yet seen similar traction with Red Canary (as the company looks to incorporate capabilities into Zero Trust Exchange), making it difficult to see the platform story really playing out for now. That puts focus squarely on ZIA/ZPA [Zscaler Internet Access/Zscaler Private Access], which are growing in the mid-teens Y/Y, challenging the ability to sustain 20%+ growth rate once Red Canary becomes inorganic in FY27," said Marshall and her team.

data.title: Musk fraud claims against Altman, OpenAI dismissed
data.description:
• A California federal judge on Friday dismissed fraud claims alleged by Elon Musk that OpenAI, the artificial intelligence company he helped found, abandoned its founding mission to operate as a non-profit organization.
  • However, a trial will still start on April 27 to address the Tesla (TSLA) CEO's breach of charitable trust and unjust ​enrichment claims against OpenAI and co-founder Sam Altman.
  • Musk has argued that the company turned its back on its origins when it accepted a multi-billion-dollar investment from Microsoft (MSFT), which currently has a 27% stake, as well as creating a for-profit entity in 2019, after he left the OpenAI board.
  • He is asking for a court order to change OpenAI's status back to a non-profit and also that Altman and company President Greg Brockman lose those positions.
  • Musk is also seeking $150B in damages, according to Reuters. If successful, those proceeds would go to OpenAI's charitable division.

data.title: American Express, Bank of America, Mastercard in losers: week's financials wrap
data.description:
The State Street Financial Sel Sec SPDR ETF (XLF) closed the trading week 1.01% lower at $51.42.

Investors evaluated the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension of the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

Meanwhile, earnings releases continued to impact stock movements during the week.

Among megacap stocks, American Express (AXP) led the losers, retreating 5.31% to close the week at $314.08.

The credit card company's Q1 earnings and revenue topped Wall Street consensus estimates as its cardholders continued to spend. Network volume and billed business growth increased from the prior quarter's pace.

However, provision for credit losses jumped ~9% from the prior year.

Bank of America (BAC) was the next on the list, falling 3.45% to close at $52.05.

Mastercard (MA) (-3.29% to $504.17) was the next major loser. MA is set to report its quarterly financial results next week.

Goldman Sachs Group (GS) was the only gainer in the category, advancing 0.10% to $926.91. The week saw the banking giant agree to settle a class action lawsuit that accused it of defrauding shareholders about its work for 1MDB, the Malaysian sovereign wealth fund at the center of a multibillion-dollar scandal.

For largecap stocks, Woori Financial (WF) (-9.66% W/W to $67.14) and Nomura Holdings (NMR) (-8.59% W/W to $7.87) led the weekly decliners after posting their quarterly results.

Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria (BBVA) (-8.30% W/W to $21.87) was the next major loser in the category.

SEI Investments (SEIC) (+11.34% to $88.84) was a notable gainer. The company posted a Q1 EPS and revenue beat this week.

Figure Technology Solutions (FIGR) (-12.33% W/W to $32.15), Sprott (SII) (-11.93% W/W to $130.69), and Banco BBVA Argentina (BBAR) (-11.86% W/W to $14.43) led the midcap losers.

Meanwhile, Washington Trust Bancorp (WASH) (-14.35% W/W to $30.81) and NB Bancorp (NBBK) (-11.91% W/W to $19.82) were the notable smallcap losers, having posted disappointing Q1 earnings and revenue.

BitFuFu (FUFU) was a major decliner, retreating 14.17% W/W to $2.12.

data.title: Senate banking committee vote on Warsh set for April 29
data.description:
• The Senate Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee will vote on whether to advance the nomination of Kevin Warsh as the next Federal Reserve chairman on Wednesday, April 29.
  • The executive session, which will take place at 10a ET on that day, was scheduled late on Friday.
  • "No Republican claiming to care about Fed independence should support moving forward the nomination of Kevin Warsh, who proved in his nomination hearing to be nothing more than President Trump’s sock puppet," committee Ranking Member Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) said in a statement.
  • Current Fed Chair Jerome Powell's term ends on May 15.

More on the Federal Reserve

  • Senate banking Democrats want more info from Warsh on his finances

  • Warsh declares Fed policy independence as essential and urges Fed to stay in its lane
  • Trump says he will fire Fed's Powell if he doesn't leave 'on time'
  • Fed officials see higher risk in inflation and labor market, while the Iran war clouds outlook: FOMC minutes
